title: Confessions of an English Opium-Eater
description: Confessions of an English Opium-Eater — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: "Navigational table of contents for the 17 sections of Confessions of an English Opium-Eater."

memes.label: "Anastasius"
memes.description: "Thomas Hope's sprawling Orientalist novel (1819) — cited by De Quincey as the source of the Turkish word 'madjoon' for opium during the Malay incident, a minor but revealing literary reference that illuminates the cultural availability of Orientalist knowledge in the Romantic period."

memes.label: "Anastasius and the Confessional Mode"
memes.description: "How Thomas Hope's *Anastasius* (1819) served as both source and precursor to De Quincey's confessional mode — the intersection of Orientalist fiction and autobiographical drug narrative in Romantic prose."

memes.label: "Ann"
memes.description: "The young prostitute who befriended the 16-year-old De Quincey during his London poverty in 1802 — her sudden disappearance became the emotional core of the Confessions and a figure of irrecoverable loss that haunts both the waking narrative and the opium dreams."

memes.label: "Antiquities of Rome (Piranesi)"
memes.description: "Piranesi's engraved views of Roman antiquities — the visual works that inspired De Quincey's architectural dream imagery and provided the metaphor of infinite staircases and impossible spaces."

memes.label: "Asia"
memes.description: "A vast geographical imagination-space in the *Confessions* — not a specific place visited but a dream-region populated by Oriental imagery that haunts De Quincey's opium visions in the later sections."

memes.label: "Awsiter (Apothecary)"
memes.description: "The apothecary to Greenwich Hospital whose 1763 'Essay on the Effects of Opium' De Quincey cites as an example of medical ignorance — a representative figure in De Quincey's polemic against the medical establishment."

memes.label: "Bath"
memes.description: "The spa city on De Quincey's travel route — a point on the geographical network connecting London, Bristol, and Wales in his early wanderings."

memes.label: "Bristol"
memes.description: "The port city connected to De Quincey's narrative through the Bristol mail coach — a transit point and geographical reference in his wanderings between Wales and London."

memes.label: "British Museum"
memes.description: "The British Museum in London — referenced by De Quincey as a public institution housing classical antiquities; mentioned as the home of the Piranesi engravings that shaped his opium dream imagery."

memes.label: "Cambridge"
memes.description: "The university city linked to De Quincey's social circle — mentioned in connection with Eton and the network of elite educational institutions."

memes.label: "Carceri d'Invenzione"
memes.description: "Piranesi's series of etching 'Imaginary Prisons' (first edition 1745, second 1761) — the most direct visual source for De Quincey's descriptions of architectural dream-spaces, though not named explicitly in the text."

memes.label: Charles Baudelaire
memes.description: French poet and critic whose *Les Paradis Artificiels* (1860) drew on De Quincey's *Confessions* and helped transmit its ideas to Continental Europe.

memes.label: Charles Lamb
memes.description: English essayist and poet, a central figure in the Romantic literary circle and friend of Thomas De Quincey.

memes.label: Childhood
memes.description: A central theme in De Quincey's Confessions, where early childhood experiences are presented as formative for later psychological development.

memes.label: "China"
memes.description: "In De Quincey's opium visions, China appears as a land of ancient grandeur and alien immensity — a key site of the orientalist dream-imagery that structures the later sections of the Confessions."

memes.label: "Royal College of Surgeons"
memes.description: "The medical institution in London whose 'official historian' De Quincey polemically refutes in the Confessions — representing the institutional medical authority against which he positions his personal testimony on opium."

memes.label: "Confession as Genre"
memes.description: "The literary form that De Quincey adopts — a confessional narrative that frames opium-eating as a moral and philosophical problem, blending autobiography, medical treatise, and Romantic prose."

memes.label: "Confessions of an English Opium-Eater"
memes.description: "De Quincey's masterwork — first published in the *London Magazine* (September–October 1821), expanded into book form in 1822 and further revised for the collected edition of 1856. A foundational text of autobiographical prose, drug literature, and Romantic confessional narrative, structured around the author's experience as an opium-eater."

memes.label: "D——, the Jewish Money-Lender"
memes.description: "An anonymous Jewish money-lender in London to whom the young De Quincey repeatedly turned for financial advances against his future expectations — a figure whose transactions frame the author's early struggles with debt."

memes.label: "David Ricardo"
memes.description: "The British political economist (1772–1823) whose work De Quincey engages with in the economic digressions of the *Confessions* — representing the intersection of literature and political economy in De Quincey's intellectual world."

memes.label: De Quincey at Oxford
memes.description: De Quincey's years at Worcester College, Oxford (1803–1808), where his opium use intensified and his scholarly reputation flourished.

memes.label: "Collected Works of De Quincey"
memes.description: "Thomas De Quincey's collected writings (first collected edition 1853–60) — the broader oeuvre of essays on philosophy, political economy, and literary criticism that form the intellectual context for his most famous work, the *Confessions of an English Opium-Eater*."

memes.label: "Dr. William Buchan"
memes.description: "Scottish physician (1729–1805) and author of Domestic Medicine (1769), the popular household health manual that De Quincey humorously cites as his medical authority for a moderate opium regimen."

memes.label: "Easter Sunday"
memes.description: "A recurring temporal and religious reference point in the Confessions — Easter Sunday appears at key moments in the dream sections, associated with themes of resurrection, childhood, grief, and the passage of time."

memes.label: Edgar Allan Poe
memes.description: American writer, poet, and literary critic, whose exploration of psychological extremes was influenced by De Quincey's Confessions.

memes.label: Edinburgh
memes.description: Capital city of Scotland, referenced in the *Confessions* as the source of a medical friend's correspondence regarding opium.

memes.label: "England"
memes.description: "The national frame of the entire *Confessions* — the cultural, legal, and social context within which De Quincey's opium-eating takes place and against which he measures the exoticism of Oriental opium culture."

memes.label: "English Mountains"
memes.description: "The rural setting of De Quincey's cottage where the Malay incident occurred — a space of domestic retreat contrasted with the urban intensity of London."

memes.label: English Opium Trade
memes.description: The commercial importation and sale of opium in early nineteenth-century Britain, the context for De Quincey's easy access to the drug.

memes.label: "Essay on the Effects of Opium"
memes.description: "Awsiter's medical treatise (1763) — cited and challenged by De Quincey as representative of the medical establishment's ignorance about opium, a foil against which he positions his own experiential authority."

memes.label: "Eton"
memes.description: "The elite school whose 'Etonian brilliancy' De Quincey contrasts with his own wandering youth — an educational institution that represents both privilege and the path not taken."

memes.label: Euripides
memes.description: Ancient Greek tragedian; cited by De Quincey for his representation of intense suffering in drama.

memes.label: "First Opium Purchase"
memes.description: "The fateful autumn Sunday in 1804 when De Quincey, suffering from rheumatic pain, purchased a tincture of opium from a druggist on Oxford Street — the event that inaugurated nearly two decades of opium-eating and launched one of the most famous confessions in English literature."

memes.label: Flight from School
memes.description: The adolescent De Quincey's dramatic escape from Manchester Grammar School in 1802, which began his period of wandering through Wales and London.

memes.label: "Geoffrey Chaucer"
memes.description: "The medieval English poet (c.1343–1400) referenced by De Quincey as a foundational figure in English literature — representing the longue durée of the literary tradition De Quincey positions himself within."

memes.label: "German Metaphysics"
memes.description: "The tradition of post-Kantian German philosophy—principally Kant, Fichte, and Schelling—that De Quincey studied intensively during his opium years and which he regarded as a defining element of his intellectual identity. (009-169)"

memes.label: "Giuseppina Grassini"
memes.description: "Italian contralto who sang at the London Opera during De Quincey's opium years; her voice was, he writes, 'delightful to me beyond all that I had ever heard.' (008-155)"

memes.label: Grasmere
memes.description: Village in the Lake District where De Quincey lived at Dove Cottage (formerly Wordsworth's home) and where the Malay encounter took place.

memes.label: Great Titchfield Street
memes.description: London street near Oxford Street where De Quincey arranged to reunite with Ann — a meeting that never took place.

memes.label: Homer
memes.description: The legendary ancient Greek epic poet, author of the *Iliad* and *Odyssey*; alluded to by De Quincey in his dream narratives.

memes.label: "Horace Walpole (Lord Orford)"
memes.description: "The English writer and art historian (1717–1797), referred to as Lord Orford in the text — a figure from the preceding generation of English letters whose works De Quincey's readers would have recognised."

memes.label: "Hounslow"
memes.description: "A town west of London on the Bath Road, whose heath was notorious for highway robberies — the setting for a key transitional passage in De Quincey's early wanderings and a potent symbol of the vulnerability he experienced in the London hinterland."

memes.label: "The Iliad"
memes.description: "Homer's epic poem — from which De Quincey quotes Greek lines in his comic attempt to communicate with the Malay visitor, representing the classical education and literary sensibility that permeates the *Confessions*."

memes.label: "Immanuel Kant"
memes.description: "The German philosopher (1724–1804) whose works De Quincey reports reading and understanding under opium's influence — a figure representing the intellectual ambition that opium allegedly supported rather than undermined."

memes.label: "Intoxication"
memes.description: "The state of alcohol-induced intoxication that De Quincey uses as a foil to explain opium's fundamentally different effects — a key distinction in his defence of opium-eating."

memes.label: "Jeremy Taylor"
memes.description: "The 17th-century Anglican divine and author whose prose style De Quincey admired and referenced — a figure from the devotional literary tradition that influenced the confessional mode."

memes.label: John Keats
memes.description: English Romantic poet, known for his odes and sensual imagery.

memes.label: John Milton
memes.description: English poet and intellectual, author of *Paradise Lost*; repeatedly cited by De Quincey in the *Confessions* for his depictions of sublime spaces and suffering.

memes.label: Jonas Hanway
memes.description: English philanthropist, traveller, and writer; invoked by De Quincey as an opponent of tea-drinking in contrast with Dr. Johnson.

memes.label: Lake District
memes.description: The mountainous region of northwest England that became the spiritual home of the Romantic poets — Wordsworth, Coleridge, Southey, and later De Quincey.

memes.label: "Lake District"
memes.description: "The mountainous region of northwestern England associated with Wordsworth and Coleridge — the landscape that formed the backdrop to English Romanticism and the setting of De Quincey's cottage during his years of opium consumption."

memes.label: "Laudanum"
memes.description: "The alcoholic tincture of opium — the specific form in which De Quincey consumed the drug, distinguished from crude opium by its intoxicating spirit content."

memes.label: Person Index
memes.description: Complete index of all person entries in the wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: Place Index
memes.description: Complete index of all places referenced in the wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: Work Index
memes.description: Complete index of all literary and artistic works referenced in the wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: "Liverpool"
memes.description: "The northern port city mentioned in De Quincey's economic and geographical references — part of the network of English cities that formed the backdrop to his early life."

memes.label: Livy
memes.description: Roman historian whose account of the Second Punic War is cited by De Quincey in the *Confessions* as a benchmark for tragic magnitude.

memes.label: "London"
memes.description: "The primary urban setting of the Confessions — the city where De Quincey first encountered opium, wandered the streets in poverty, met and lost Ann, and experienced both the sublime pleasures and abyssal pains of his opium life."

memes.label: "London Magazine"
memes.description: "The monthly periodical in which the *Confessions* first appeared in two instalments (September and October 1821) — the publication venue that shaped the text's initial reception, its readership, and its positioning within early 19th-century periodical culture."

memes.label: Lord Byron
memes.description: The most famous of the English Romantic poets, a contemporary of De Quincey whose life and works defined the Byronic hero archetype.

memes.label: "M. (De Quincey's Wife)"
memes.description: "Margaret De Quincey, the author's wife, referred to in the text as 'beloved M.' and compared to Electra — the devoted companion who 'defrauded herself of sleep' to nurse him through the worst of his opium sufferings."

memes.label: "Malay Incident"
memes.description: "The enigmatic visit of an Asiatic traveller to De Quincey's cottage — a brief real-world encounter that becomes a recurring nightmare figure in the opium dreams, exemplifying the transformative power of opium on memory and imagination."

memes.label: "The Malay's Visit"
memes.description: "The brief but unforgettable visit of an anonymous Malay traveller to De Quincey's cottage near Grasmere — a real encounter that became, through opium's transformative power, a recurring archetype of Oriental terror in the author's dreams."

memes.label: "The Malay (Visitor)"
memes.description: "The anonymous Asiatic traveller whose visit to De Quincey's cottage near Grasmere became one of the most famous episodes in the *Confessions* — a real person transformed by opium into a recurrent archetype of Oriental terror in the author's dreams."

memes.label: "Manchester"
memes.description: "The industrial city referenced in De Quincey's economic discussions — a node in the commercial geography of early 19th-century England that contextualises his political-economic observations."

memes.label: "Melancholy"
memes.description: "The profound depressive state that both drove De Quincey to opium and was paradoxically intensified by it — the emotional axis on which the entire Confessions narrative pivots."

memes.label: "Memory"
memes.description: "The cognitive faculty that opium transforms and amplifies — resurrecting long-buried childhood scenes with hallucinatory precision in the dream-state, and thereby linking the personal past to the universal themes of the Confessions."

memes.label: "Mithridates (Adelung)"
memes.description: "Johann Christoph Adelung's linguistic reference work (1806) — mentioned by De Quincey in a comic aside during the Malay incident as a book that 'might have helped me to a few words' of Malay."

memes.label: "Mrs. Betty"
memes.description: "De Quincey's landlady during his Welsh sojourn, a former servant of the Bishop of Bangor, whose inadvertent revelation of the bishop's suspicions precipitated De Quincey's departure and subsequent financial distress."

memes.label: "Music"
memes.description: "The aesthetic experience that De Quincey analyses in a philosophical digression — music as an intellectual pleasure amplified by opium, exemplifying the drug's capacity to enhance the mind's active engagement with art."

memes.label: "Dejection: An Ode"
memes.description: "Coleridge's confessional ode (1802) — with its themes of melancholy and creative paralysis, a work that shares the psychological terrain of the *Confessions* and represents the Lake Poets' engagement with altered states of consciousness."

memes.label: "Opium"
memes.description: "The central psychoactive substance of De Quincey's autobiographical narrative — a celestial drug of unparalleled pleasures and pains that defines the Confessions, consumed by the author for nearly two decades from 1804."

memes.label: Opium Addiction
memes.description: The physiological and psychological dependence on opium that De Quincey chronicles in the *Confessions*, including its cycles of pleasure, tolerance, and suffering.

memes.label: Opium and Creativity
memes.description: The controversial relationship between opium use and artistic creativity, a central theme of De Quincey's *Confessions* and its literary afterlife.

memes.label: Opium and Memory
memes.description: The relationship between opium consumption and the intensification of memory — one of De Quincey's most original contributions to the psychology of drug experience.

memes.label: "Opium and Orientalism"
memes.description: "The intersection of opium, colonial trade, and British Orientalist attitudes in De Quincey's *Confessions* — centred on the Malay visitor episode (010-109–132) as a touchpoint for 19th-century British perceptions of the East."

memes.label: "Opium and Wine"
memes.description: "De Quincey's sustained comparison between opium and wine — the central rhetorical device through which he elevates opium-eating above mere intoxication and constructs the opium-eater as a figure of rational pleasure rather than degraded indulgence."

memes.label: "Opium Dreams"
memes.description: "The vivid, architectonic oneiric experiences induced by prolonged opium use — the phantasmagoria that dominates Sections 12–14 of the Confessions and constitutes its most influential contribution to Romantic and Gothic literature."

memes.label: "Opium-Eating"
memes.description: "The cultural practice of regular opium consumption as documented and exemplified by De Quincey — a habit he distinguishes from medicinal use and alcohol consumption alike."

memes.label: Opium Visions
memes.description: The elaborate architectural and spatial hallucinations described by De Quincey in the later sections of the *Confessions*, which he distinguished from ordinary dreams.

memes.label: Opium Withdrawal
memes.description: The physical and psychological syndrome of opium cessation, described by De Quincey with clinical precision in the final sections of the *Confessions*.

memes.label: "Orientalism in the Confessions"
memes.description: "The cultural framework of 19th-century British attitudes toward 'the East' that De Quincey both inherits and deploys — transforming the Malay from a tired traveller into a legion of nightmare figures and making Asia itself the theatre of opium terror."

memes.label: "Oxford"
memes.description: "The university city where De Quincey studied at Worcester College (1803–1808) — a symbol of intellectual aspiration and privilege that stands in stark contrast to the London of his poverty and opium life."

memes.label: "Oxford Street"
memes.description: "The London thoroughfare immortalised by De Quincey — the site of his first opium purchase near 'the stately Pantheon,' his nighttime wanderings with Ann, and the enduring symbol of loss and searching in the Confessions."

memes.label: "Pains of Opium"
memes.description: "The dark counterpart to opium's pleasures — the physical torments, psychological nightmares, and spiritual desolation that prolonged opium use inflicts, documented in Sections 12–14 of the Confessions."

memes.label: "Pantheon (Oxford Street)"
memes.description: "The London landmark near which De Quincey purchased his first opium — 'the stately Pantheon' as Wordsworth called it, a specific architectural anchor in the geography of the *Confessions*."

memes.label: "Paradise Regained"
memes.description: "John Milton's epic poem (1671) — the sequel to *Paradise Lost*, referenced by De Quincey as a literary touchstone and a source of allusions that resonate with the confessional and redemptive themes of his own narrative."

memes.label: Percy Bysshe Shelley
memes.description: English Romantic poet, radical thinker, and contemporary of De Quincey who is cited in the *Confessions* on the corrupting effects of old age.

memes.label: "Piccadilly"
memes.description: "The great London thoroughfare where De Quincey walked with Ann — a street whose 'tumult and blaze' forms the theatrical backdrop to one of the Confessions' most emotionally charged passages."

memes.label: "Giovanni Battista Piranesi"
memes.description: "The Italian engraver (1720–1778) whose *Carceri d'Invenzione* (Imaginary Prisons) and *Antiquities of Rome* inspired De Quincey's descriptions of architectural dream-spaces — a visual artist whose work became a crucial metaphor for the opium dream experience."

memes.label: Plato
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher; cited by De Quincey in the *Confessions* for his doctrine of recollection (anamnesis) in connection with memory and opium.

memes.label: "Pleasures of Opium"
memes.description: "The ecstatic first phase of opium experience documented in Section 7 — a state of intellectual clarity, aesthetic appreciation, and serene well-being that De Quincey presents as opium's legitimate and defensible contribution to human happiness."

memes.label: "Principles of Political Economy"
memes.description: "David Ricardo's major work (1817) — the economic treatise to which De Quincey alludes in his digressions, representing the political-economic discourse that the *Confessions* occasionally engages as intellectual context."

memes.label: "Religio Medici"
memes.description: "Sir Thomas Browne's spiritual autobiography (1643) — cited by De Quincey in his digression on music as containing one of the few adequate discussions of that subject in all literature, and as a model of the confessional-meditative mode."

memes.label: Robert Southey
memes.description: English Romantic poet, historian, and Poet Laureate; contemporary of Wordsworth, Coleridge, and De Quincey.

memes.label: Romantic Autobiography
memes.description: The literary genre of personal confession and self-examination that flourished in the Romantic period, of which the *Confessions of an English Opium-Eater* is a defining example.

memes.label: "Romantic Opium Culture"
memes.description: "The network of Romantic-era writers who used opium — centred on De Quincey and Coleridge — and how their shared drug experience shaped confessional prose, dream-writing, and 19th-century literary culture."

memes.label: "Samson Agonistes"
memes.description: "Milton's tragic closet drama (1671) — published in the same volume as *Paradise Regained* and referenced by De Quincey as part of the Miltonic literary universe that informs the *Confessions*."

memes.label: "Dr. Samuel Johnson"
memes.description: "The 18th-century literary giant (1709–1784) whom De Quincey references as an authority and cultural touchstone — a figure representing the English literary tradition within which De Quincey positions his own work."

memes.label: "Samuel Taylor Coleridge"
memes.description: "The Lake Poet and fellow opium-user whose experience with the drug De Quincey references as both parallel and contrast to his own — a figure who represents the intersection of opium, creativity, and suffering in Romantic literature."

memes.label: Soho
memes.description: Central London district where De Quincey obtained his opium from a druggist; one of the geographical anchors of his London life.

memes.label: "Solitude"
memes.description: "The social and psychological condition De Quincey presents as both a predisposition of the opium-eater and an effect of the drug — a self-reinforcing cycle of withdrawal from society that deepens the dependence."

memes.label: Sophocles
memes.description: Ancient Greek tragedian, author of *Oedipus Rex* and *Antigone*; cited by De Quincey in the *Confessions* alongside Euripides.

memes.label: "Suffering"
memes.description: "The experiential foundation of the Confessions — physical suffering as the entry point to opium use, psychological torment as its consequence, and the narrative of endurance that frames the entire work as both confession and vindication."

memes.label: The Lost Ann
memes.description: The disappearance of Ann, the young prostitute who befriended De Quincey during his London poverty, and his fruitless search for her on Oxford Street.

memes.label: "Thomas De Quincey"
memes.description: "English essayist and literary critic (1785–1859), author of *Confessions of an English Opium-Eater* (1821) — one of the most influential works of Romantic prose and a foundational text in the literature of altered consciousness, addiction memoir, and dream-writing."

memes.label: Thomas Hope
memes.description: British writer, art collector, and author of the novel Anastasius, which De Quincey admired.

memes.label: Turkey
memes.description: "A key source region for the opium trade referenced in the Confessions — De Quincey distinguishes between Turkey opium and East Indian opium in his account of prices, quality, and availability. (007-165)"

memes.label: "Twelfth Night"
memes.description: "Shakespeare's comedy (c. 1601) — cited by De Quincey in his music digression as containing 'the fine extravaganza' on the subject of music, one of the few adequate treatments of the topic in literature."

memes.label: "Wales"
memes.description: "The region where De Quincey's early wanderings took place — a space of poverty, subsistence, and the pre-opium phase of his life that the Confessions' introductory narrative recounts."

memes.label: "Sir Walter Raleigh"
memes.description: "The English explorer and writer (c.1552–1618) referenced as a historical figure in De Quincey's wide-ranging cultural references — part of the tapestry of English history that the *Confessions* draws upon."

memes.label: Westminster
memes.description: The political heart of London, referenced in the *Confessions* as the seat of Parliament and a symbol of public life contrasted with De Quincey's private suffering.

memes.label: William Harriott
memes.description: London magistrate and author of *Struggles through Life*, cited by De Quincey for his experiment with laudanum for the gout.

memes.label: William Hazlitt
memes.description: English writer, critic, and essayist of the Romantic period, known for his humanistic essays and literary criticism.

memes.label: "William Wordsworth"
memes.description: "The Lake Poet and neighbour whose description of London ('the stately Pantheon') De Quincey quotes — a towering literary figure whose presence shadows the *Confessions*."

memes.label: Worcester College, Oxford
memes.description: The Oxford college where Thomas De Quincey studied (though he does not name it explicitly in the *Confessions*, referring to it obliquely as "—— College, Oxford").
